customManagers.description: biome.json carries the config schema version inside its $schema URL, and no manager reads that file — so a @biomejs/biome bump in package.json leaves the URL behind (it sat at 2.5.4 while the package was already 2.5.5, which silently validates the config against the wrong schema in the editor). This extracts the version from the URL as the same dep the npm manager already sees, so both updates share a branch and land in one PR. depTypeTemplate is what puts it under the devDependencies automerge rule below: without a depType it matches nothing, and an un-automergeable update sitting in the branch would block the automerge of the biome PR itself.

packageRules.description: One dependency per PR. Renovate groups by default via config:recommended (all @types together, all non-major devDependencies together), which makes a single PR carry several unrelated upgrades: a red CI then names the group rather than the package that broke it, and reverting takes the innocent ones with it. `null` cancels an inherited groupName, so this rule has to sit first and every deliberate grouping below has to re-declare its own.

Majors wait for an explicit tick on the Dependency Dashboard instead of opening a PR on their own. A major is the one update that can need a code change to land, so it should arrive when someone is ready to look at it, not sit open going stale. The Renovate workflow reads this same Pending Approval section and notifies Telegram, which is what stops the list being one nobody reads.
Automerge non-major devDependency updates once CI is green — one PR each.
@types/vscode must stay in sync with engines.vscode (the minimum supported VS Code version). Never bump it automatically — raise engines.vscode by hand first.

engines.vscode is a floor: it declares the oldest VS Code that may install the extension. Renovate resolves it from github-tags on microsoft/vscode, so every hotfix tag is a candidate and #38 landed ^1.129.1 — which excludes anyone still on 1.129.0 for a patch that, by VS Code's release model, carries no new API at all. API only ever arrives on a minor, so the floor only ever needs to move to a minor. allowedVersions keeps just the x.y.0 tags in play; every minor from 1.98 up has one, so this narrows the target rather than blocking the update. It has to be matchDepNames: the npm manager rewrites engines.vscode to packageName microsoft/vscode and leaves depName as vscode, and since Renovate 39 matchPackageNames only ever looks at packageName — the depName fallback (with its 'Use matchDepNames instead' warning) is gone. So `"matchPackageNames": ["vscode"]` matched nothing, the whole rule was inert, and #3 landed ^1.129.1 exactly like #38. The floor may follow VS Code, but late and never by itself: minimumReleaseAge holds a fresh minor back a month, automerge is off and needs-review marks it as a human call. Unattended it had climbed to ^1.129.0 while @types/vscode sat at 1.125.0 (that one is enabled: false), shipping an extension nobody between the two could install — a floor above the API level actually compiled against costs users and buys nothing, so whoever reviews the PR raises @types/vscode in the same breath or closes it.
@types/node describes the Node runtime VS Code embeds, not the one we develop on. A major bump can start type-checking against APIs the host does not ship, so it is reviewed by hand.

The one grouping that survives, and not for convenience: the runtime and the grammar are ABI-coupled, and dist/*.wasm is copied from both packages by esbuild.mjs. A PR that moves one without the other ships a runtime that cannot load the grammar it was built against, so splitting them would produce a broken PR by construction. Reviewed by hand, never automerged.

The extension host loads dist/*.wasm copied from these packages by esbuild.mjs, so a bundler bump is a packaging change. CI packages a VSIX, which is the check that matters. `minor` is listed because esbuild is still 0.x: semver calls 0.25→0.28 a minor, so a major-only rule would never fire for it and every breaking bundler release would automerge as a devDependency.
Keep GitHub Actions current and automerge them, one Action per PR. Digests are pinned by config:best-practices, so a bump is a reviewed SHA move.
